title: A resolution memorializing those lost to the COVID-19 pandemic.
plain_summary: This bill is about remembering people who died from COVID-19. It wants to honor those who lost their lives during the pandemic and recognize how much pain their families and communities felt. The bill supports creating a special day every year on the first Monday in March to remember all the COVID-19 victims. This would help make sure we don't forget the people we lost during this difficult time.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions. (text: CR S1610)

sections.title: Resolution Memorializing Those Lost to the COVID-19 Pandemic
sections.text: The first Monday in March is known as COVID-19 Victims Memorial Day. SARS-CoV-2 is a type of coronavirus that causes the disease called COVID-19. In late two thousand nineteen, COVID-19 started spreading around the world and became a pandemic that badly hurt human life, communities, and the economy in the United States. In March two thousand twenty, communities across America began seeing more deaths because of COVID-19. Families lost parents, brothers and sisters, children, friends, and neighbors to this virus. Starting in two thousand twenty, many people in America were personally affected by COVID-19 and continue to be affected. This includes mourning loved ones who died and dealing with long-term health problems from the virus that doctors still do not fully understand. By the end of February two thousand twenty-five, there were more than one hundred three million known cases of COVID-19 in the United States. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention estimates that more than one million two hundred twenty thousand people sadly died from illness caused by COVID-19. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention estimates that between October first two thousand twenty-four and February fifteenth two thousand twenty-five, there were six million four hundred thousand to eleven million five hundred thousand known cases of COVID-19 in the United States. The COVID-19 pandemic hurt some groups of people more than others. This includes people with low incomes, communities of color, people with disabilities, people with weak immune systems, people with other health problems, and people living in group settings like nursing homes and prisons. Workers on the front lines, essential workers, and healthcare workers made selfless sacrifices to protect their neighbors and communities, support local businesses that were struggling, and find new ways to provide services. Local, state, tribal, and federal government agencies provided important help to businesses, communities, and people in need across the United States. Each life lost to COVID-19 and each sacrifice made should never be forgotten. Therefore, the Senate decides that it will honor and remember those who died from COVID-19. The Senate recognizes the suffering of people who got the SARS-CoV-2 virus and those who continue to struggle with ongoing effects of COVID-19. The Senate supports making the first Monday in March an official annual day called COVID-19 Victims Memorial Day.
